text: one wants you! Just go!” she shouted, and his ears twitched as he started back toward her. She lifted her lawn chair overhead with a threatening squeal, and though Frankie continued to the house, he took a sharp turn and headed toward the cellar. She followed him, chair still overhead, and he picked up his pace, jogging out of sight. He was gone by the time she reached the small hole in the foundation, and she dropped the chair with a frustrated sigh. “You want to hang out with the worms and rot, fine. See if I care.” As she dragged the chair back to the house, the light shining from the kitchen filled her with sickening dread. She broke into a run, but not fast enough to stop Biscuit from dashing out the open door. Lisa screamed for him to stop, but the tiny cat scuttled joyfully out of reach and into the dark. He was still so small, so helpless. Frankie could take care of himself—not that she much cared either way—but Biscuit wouldn’t stand a chance against the coyotes that roamed the countryside at night. With every light on full blast, Lisa ran around the backyard, calling for Biscuit until her throat was raw. Hoping for as little as a mew in response, she shook treats and opened can after can of expensive tuna as she scoured the brush and tree line, kissing the air so many times her lips went numb. Her Facebook groups were no help, though she had to pretend they were so no one would know she still hadn’t found him eight hours later. Or heard a single cry. He was quiet for so long she thought maybe he’d started running and never stopped. But in the deepest part of night, when she’d passed out in her lawn chair and moths had come to settle on her arms, a strained yowl permeated her dreams. She hadn’t known Biscuit long, but she knew it was him calling for her. She searched the perimeter of the house and cafe again, then ventured into the woods and down to the stream, all the while shouting, whistling, and begging for some sign that Biscuit was okay. But the cry never got closer. It got more urgent though, strained and thin, and as the sky lightened with streaks of pink, the howl crescendoed to a scream that rivaled any she’d heard in the worst shelters. Back in the house, the sound continued with new zest. It felt like a living thing that hunkered down so heavily in her chest she couldn't take a full breath. She tried to sleep, but the yowling, which continued almost nonstop for the next three days, kept her from anything but shallow unconsciousness. It evolved too. Only a minuscule layer of the noise reminded her of a meow; the rest was an alien sort of howling and grinding that didn’t sound biological, let alone feline. The noise affected every creature in the house adjoined to the Ninth Life Café. After the first full day of screams, most of the house-cats stopped venturing below the second floor—many even took to hiding out in the attic where the noise was softest, leaving Lisa to suffer the torturous cries all alone. She played music at full volume throughout the house and cafe, but even the earsplitting soprano of Renee Fleming couldn’t dampen the kitten’s agonized cry. It felt like it was always just behind her, or underneath her, or inside her. She prodded her belly as if Biscuit was tangled up in her guts and laughed. The audience laughed too. They applauded as she spun in place, her hands in her hair and body aching with exhaustion. Charlie used to tell her all the time she needed to rest. “I don’t need rest. I need to keep my dream alive.” To which he’d nod and say, “Whatever you think is best.” But Lisa knew he didn’t believe it. He didn’t care what she thought or felt. He didn’t care what she said or did, just as long as she left him alone to be perfectly content with air in his lungs. Much good all that air did him now. That’s when she realized the answer to her problem. The only way to escape an unstoppable noise was through an untouchably deep sleep. And she knew just how to get it. Charlie’s liquor cabinet had gone largely ignored since he passed away—the cafe kept Lisa too busy for alcohol, even to relax. Charlie hadn’t been a big drinker, either, which annoyed her more than she knew it should have. In her experience, men with so few responsibilities, hobbies, or interests outside the home usually latched onto intoxicants to fill the hours, but not Charlie. He wasn’t even interesting enough to be a drunk. She quickly discovered she didn’t enjoy liquor, but seeing as enjoyment wasn’t the goal, she forced glass upon glass down her throat until, after what felt like days of binge drinking, a friendly oblivion dropped a rose-colored curtain over her eyes, and the kitten’s cries of agony stopped. Lisa’s began soon after. # She awoke broken at the bottom of the foyer stairs, soaked in blood and vomit. Everything hurt, every piece of her felt dislocated, but not as insistently as her left leg. Her vision was bleary, but she could tell her leg was bent the wrong way, and the colors were...off. She noted it casually, like when the cafe ran out of whole milk. Though an important issue, it didn’t seem as pressing as the small, mutilated creature standing in the hallway, licking its chops. From a ruptured eye, glistening sludge ran like a crusty river of black tears down its face, and each wobbly step caused a new lump of flesh to drop off its exposed ribs.
